name: London Hammer
description: The London Hammer, also known as the London Artifact, is a purported anomalous artifact discovered in London, Texas, in 1936. Proponents claim it is an ancient hammer embedded in Ordovician rock, suggesting advanced technology predating known human history. The artifact consists of an iron hammerhead and a partially petrified wooden handle encased in a concretion of rock. The core claim is that the rock formation surrounding the hammer is millions of years old, implying the hammer itself is of similar age. This challenges conventional timelines of human technological development. The artifact gained prominence in creationist circles, who use it to argue against evolutionary theory and support the idea of a young Earth. Mainstream geology and archaeology reject the London Hammer as evidence of ancient civilizations or advanced prehistoric technology. Geologists point out that the rock concretion is likely a relatively recent formation, possibly created by mineral-rich water depositing around the hammer. Iron implements can accelerate such concretion formation. The hammer itself is consistent with 19th-century American mining tools. Microscopic analysis has revealed that the hammer is a common tool from the late 1800s to early 1900s. The limestone surrounding the hammer could have formed quickly around a discarded tool. While the hammer is undeniably real, its discovery in a rock formation does not necessitate a prehistoric origin. The artifact's popularity stems from its appeal to those seeking evidence that contradicts established scientific understanding of geological timescales and human history. The artifact is housed in the Creation Evidence Museum in Glen Rose, Texas. There is no scientific evidence to suggest that the hammer is anything other than a relatively modern tool found encased in a more recently formed rock concretion.
subtitle: Anomalous hammer from Texas rock.

claims.claim: The hammer is millions of years old.
claims.evidence: The hammer is embedded in Ordovician rock (claimed to be 400-500 million years old).
claims.criticism: The rock concretion formed relatively recently, likely around a discarded hammer. The rock type is not definitively Ordovician.

claims.claim: The hammer proves advanced prehistoric technology.
claims.evidence: If the hammer is millions of years old, it demonstrates human or other intelligent beings had advanced technology far earlier than current understanding.
claims.criticism: The hammer's design is consistent with 19th-century tools. There's no evidence of advanced prehistoric technology. The hammer is likely a modern tool.

claims.evidence: Its existence supposedly contradicts the established timeline of human evolution and technological development.
claims.criticism: The hammer's age is not millions of years old, so it does not challenge evolutionary theory. The hammer is a modern tool, incorrectly interpreted as an ancient artifact.

keyFigures.name: Carl Baugh
keyFigures.role: Owner of the Creation Evidence Museum and promoter of the London Hammer as evidence against evolution.

keyFigures.name: Glen J. Kuban
keyFigures.role: Researcher and critic who has investigated and debunked claims about the London Hammer's age and significance.

mythologyConnections: None directly, but indirectly linked to myths of ancient advanced civilizations and lost technologies.

timeline.event: The London Hammer is discovered in London, Texas.
The hammer gains popularity within creationist circles as evidence against evolution.
The hammer continues to be displayed at the Creation Evidence Museum and debated within alternative history and scientific communities.

sources.author: Glen J. Kuban
sources.work: The London Hammer: An Alleged Out-of-Place Artifact
sources.title: Glen J. Kuban
